summary: Simon Späti argues that Generative Business Intelligence (GenBI) represents a paradigm shift in how business users interact with data, enabled by the declarative BI-as-Code approach where dashboards and metrics are defined in YAML rather than hidden in GUI configurations. The key insight is that declarative definitions provide the semantic foundation AI models need to understand business logic, relationships, and metrics. GenBI promises to overcome the traditional bottleneck of data teams by allowing natural language conversations to generate dashboards and metrics. Späti positions this as the next evolution of self-service BI, with tools like Rill Developer already implementing these capabilities through LLM-powered dashboard generation.
key_points: GenBI requires a declarative foundation - dashboards and metrics stored as code provide the semantic context AI models need to understand business logic
The metrics layer is critical infrastructure, serving as the semantic foundation that defines measures, data model relationships, and business definitions that AI can learn from
BI-as-Code enables a workflow where humans and AI collaborate through GitHub PRs, iterating on prompts until artifacts are production-ready
GenBI addresses the long-standing self-service BI challenge by enabling domain experts without SQL skills to create and iterate on dashboards through natural language
The approach scales from simple metric creation to complex data modeling, mapping conceptual dashboards through logical metrics layers to physical table joins
money_quotes: At its core, GenBI delivers an unreasonably effective human interface, where we iterate quickly, based on BI-as-Code.

Self-service BI has been around for a while, trying to enable business users to create dashboards and reports. But that transition has always been hard, and without SQL or even Python skills to wrangle and clean your data, it's hard. I think GenBI is the next attempt for Self-Service BI, which is very promising.

While AI can control visual interfaces, this approach is inefficient for complex business logic. The data model joins, metric aggregations and bus matrix require explicit declarations that AI can parse and understand; print-screened images are insufficient.
Code-first enables a smaller data team to do more. Think of the Ruby on Rails developer, who does end-to-end from changing the database theme to the front of the app.
If we can further automate this process, we will overcome one of the biggest problems of BI: the bottleneck of the data or BI teams to ship or publish data artifacts.
This is usually where technical data professionals care least, so a trained model that helps us would significantly boost productivity.
key_insight: Declarative BI-as-Code is the prerequisite for GenBI because AI models need explicit, machine-readable definitions of business logic and metrics - not opaque GUI configurations - to generate meaningful dashboards and analytics.
